content.text: Library versioning and support policy is the set of rules that govern how a shared platform library is versioned across releases, how long each version receives bug fixes and security patches, how deprecated APIs are retired, and what obligations the platform team has to consumers during transitions. Without a versioning and support policy, library releases are unpredictable, teams do not know when they must upgrade or what breaking changes to expect, and the platform team has no principled basis for deciding when to stop supporting old versions.

A versioning policy specifies the versioning scheme (semantic versioning, calendar versioning, or a hybrid) and defines precisely what constitutes a patch, minor, or major change. A support policy specifies how long each major version receives active support (full bug and security fixes), maintenance support (security fixes only), and then reaches end of life (no further patches). A deprecation policy specifies how APIs are announced as deprecated, how long they remain available before removal, and what migration guidance accompanies each deprecation.

Platform library consumers make decisions based on versioning signals. A team choosing whether to upgrade from v1.3.0 to v1.4.0 relies on the versioning contract that minor versions are backward compatible. If minor versions have historically included breaking changes, teams stop upgrading until forced, creating version lag and vulnerability accumulation. Consistent adherence to the versioning contract builds the trust that makes voluntary upgrade adoption possible.

Support windows matter for capacity planning. A team that knows v1.x reaches end of life six months after v2.0 ships can plan their migration timeline, request budget, and schedule the upgrade work. A team that does not know the support window will either upgrade reactively (under time pressure) or never (accumulating risk). Publishing clear EOL dates at the time of a major release allows teams to make informed decisions rather than discovering the constraint when the platform team announces immediate EOL.
Predictable versioning contracts enable teams to estimate upgrade effort from the version number alone.
Published EOL dates allow teams to budget and schedule migration work in advance.
Deprecation warnings in library code surface migration needs to teams during development rather than at release time.
Support tiers let the platform team maintain only the latest major version at full quality while providing security coverage for teams on the previous version.
Clear major version criteria prevent API drift from being slipped into minor releases that teams cannot practically review.
Migration guides and codemods reduce the manual effort of major version upgrades, accelerating adoption.
Strict semantic versioning requires careful judgment about what constitutes a breaking change — borderline cases require discussion and slow release cadence.
Maintaining a maintenance-support version in parallel with active development of the next major version doubles the patch workload during the overlap period.
Long deprecation windows slow the cleanup of legacy APIs, increasing the maintenance surface of the library codebase.
Publishing EOL dates creates a commitment that the platform team must honor even when priorities change.
Automated codemods for migration are expensive to write and may not cover all consumer usage patterns.

The platform team adopts semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH) with explicit definitions of each component. PATCH releases contain only bug fixes that do not change the public API. MINOR releases may add new public API surface (new functions, new optional parameters, new configuration options) but must not change or remove existing API surface in ways that break existing consumers. MAJOR releases may contain any changes including removal of deprecated APIs, parameter signature changes, or restructuring of the public interface.

Support windows are calculated from the release date of each major version. Upon release of a major version, the previous major version transitions from active to maintenance support (security fixes only) and a specific EOL date — typically six to twelve months later — is announced. This gives teams a defined migration window. The platform team backports critical security patches to the maintenance version but does not accept new feature requests or non-security bug fixes for it.

Deprecation follows a multi-step process. A new API that replaces an old one is added in a MINOR release alongside the existing API. The old API is marked deprecated in that same release using language-appropriate deprecation markers (Python `@deprecated` decorator with a message, JavaScript `@deprecated` JSDoc tag). The deprecation message specifies which version will remove the API and what the replacement is. Actual removal happens only in the next MAJOR release and only if at least two MINOR release cycles have passed since the deprecation announcement.

attrs.alt: Library versioning and support policy timeline

content.text: Define the versioning and support policy before publishing the first external release of a shared library. Teams that have already adopted a library are much harder to move to a new policy than teams who adopted the library after the policy was established. The policy should be documented in the library's README, linked from every release announcement, and referenced in the onboarding guide for teams adopting the library.

Review and update the support policy when the team or the organizational context changes significantly. A platform team that grows from two to ten engineers can commit to longer maintenance windows than a two-person team. A regulatory requirement that mandates running on versions with active security support may require shortening the transition from active to maintenance support to ensure teams migrate quickly. Policy reviews should happen at least annually and whenever a major platform change occurs.

# pyproject.toml — library packaging with Python version constraints
[tool.poetry]
name = "platform-auth"
version = "1.2.3"
description = "Internal authentication library for platform services"
authors = ["Platform Team <platform@company.internal>"]
readme = "README.md"

[tool.poetry.dependencies]
python = "^3.10"   # support policy: 3.10+ for active, document in COMPATIBILITY.md
PyJWT = "^2.8"
httpx = "^0.27"
cachetools = "^5.3"

[tool.poetry.group.dev.dependencies]
pytest = "^8.0"
pytest-asyncio = "^0.23"
respx = "^0.21"  # mock httpx in tests

[[tool.poetry.source]]
name = "internal"
url = "https://pypi.internal.company.com/simple/"
priority = "primary"

# CHANGELOG.md excerpt — structured changelog for library releases

## [2.0.0] - 2025-06-01

### Breaking Changes
- `validate_token()` removed (deprecated since 1.2.0). Use `validate_jwt()`.
- `init_auth()` now requires `audience` parameter (was optional with None default).
- Python 3.9 no longer supported (was deprecated in 1.1.0).

### Migration Guide
See [docs/migration-v1-to-v2.md](docs/migration-v1-to-v2.md) for step-by-step instructions.
A codemod script is available: `python -m platform_auth.migrate v1-to-v2 <path>`.

### Support Notice
v1.x enters maintenance support (security patches only) as of this release.
v1.x EOL date: 2025-12-01. No patches after that date.

---

## [1.2.0] - 2025-01-15

### Added
- `validate_jwt_v2()` with configurable clock skew tolerance.
- `require_scopes()` decorator as more expressive alternative to `require_roles()`.

### Deprecated
- `validate_token()` deprecated. Removed in 2.0.0. Use `validate_jwt()`.

The platform team releases platform-auth v1.2.0 with a deprecation of `validate_token()`. Services using the old function begin emitting Python DeprecationWarnings in their test suites, which surface in CI output. Teams see the warning: `validate_token is deprecated since 1.2.0 and will be removed in 2.0.0. Use validate_jwt instead.` They update their code during the next sprint, before v2.0 ships. When v2.0 arrives six months later, most consumers have already migrated; the platform team only needs to manually assist two teams that had not yet acted on the warning.

When the platform team releases v2.0.0 (a major version with breaking changes), they simultaneously publish the v1.x EOL date as December 1, 2025, and send an announcement to the library consumer channel. Teams on v1.x receive security patches until the EOL date but no feature additions. The service catalog shows which services are still on v1.x and their days-until-EOL, creating urgency for teams that have not yet migrated. Three weeks before EOL, automated reminders are sent to teams still on v1.x.

A team maintaining a service that depends on platform-auth v1.x for a compliance-controlled microservice cannot migrate to v2.0 before the EOL date due to a change freeze. They file an EOL extension request with the platform team, documenting their constraint and their planned migration date. The platform team grants a 30-day extension with the condition that the team acknowledges the security risk and commits to migrating within the extension window. This exception process prevents EOL from being a hard cliff for teams in exceptional circumstances.

The Python Software Foundation's end-of-life policy for Python itself is a well-known example of this pattern applied to a foundational library. Each Python minor version receives full support for 18 months and security-only support for an additional 3.5 years, for a total support window of five years per version. EOL dates are published years in advance on python.org/downloads, giving downstream consumers (libraries, applications, cloud services) the planning horizon they need. PEP 387 defines the deprecation policy: APIs must be deprecated in one minor release before they can be removed, with DeprecationWarnings raised on use. This policy structure — predictable versioning, published EOL dates, formal deprecation process — is the same model that internal platform teams should apply to their shared libraries.
Treating the public API contract loosely: including breaking changes in minor versions because the change 'seems small', destroying the trust that makes voluntary upgrades possible.
Not publishing EOL dates at major version release time, leaving teams uncertain about their migration urgency.
Removing deprecated APIs in the same minor release that announces the deprecation, giving teams no migration window.
Writing deprecation notices only in the changelog rather than emitting runtime warnings in the library code, so teams don't see them unless they actively monitor releases.
Failing to maintain a compatibility matrix, leaving teams to discover framework version incompatibilities by trial and error.
Granting EOL extensions without documenting the exception and extracting a firm migration commitment.
Define breaking change criteria explicitly in writing before the first release — ambiguity in the policy causes disputes and erodes team trust.
Use language-native deprecation mechanisms (Python warnings module, TypeScript @deprecated JSDoc) so deprecated APIs surface in IDE tooling and CI output.
Publish EOL dates at the moment of major version release, not after the fact.
Ship a migration guide and codemod script with every major version that has breaking changes.
Backport critical CVE patches to the maintenance support version within 48 hours of patch availability — this is the core commitment of maintenance support.
Measure and publish library adoption metrics: which version each consuming service is on, what percentage are on supported versions, trend over time.

Library versioning policy does not directly affect runtime performance. Its scalability impact is organizational: a well-designed policy scales to hundreds of library consumers because it creates predictable, low-friction upgrade paths. A poorly designed policy — one where teams do not trust that minor versions are safe to upgrade to — creates a large population of consumers pinned to old versions that require manual intervention to move. The organizational cost of managing a fragmented version distribution grows with fleet size.

The maintenance support workload scales with the number of consumers still on old versions. A platform team that keeps maintenance support windows short (six months after major release) has a bounded window of double-maintenance work. A team that extends maintenance support windows in response to consumer requests can find itself maintaining three or four major versions simultaneously, which is expensive and error-prone. Short, enforced support windows are more sustainable than open-ended extensions.
Surface in test output and IDE

Support policy directly determines the security posture of the fleet. Services running on EOL library versions may have unpatched vulnerabilities that the platform team is no longer addressing. The support policy must include a path for emergency security patches: when a critical CVE is discovered in an EOL version, the platform team should assess whether to issue a one-time emergency patch (for CVEs above a severity threshold) or to treat it as an escalated EOL notice requiring immediate migration. Document this exception process in the policy.

Version pinning by consumers is a reliability concern: services pinned to exact patch versions (e.g., `platform-auth==1.2.3`) will not receive security patches released as 1.2.4 without an explicit upgrade PR. Encourage consumers to use compatible release specifiers (`>=1.2.3,<2.0`) so that patch upgrades are automatic. Renovate bot should be configured to auto-merge patch-level library upgrades that pass CI, reducing the operational overhead of keeping services on the latest patch of their pinned minor version.

Implementing these practices effectively requires understanding both the technical and organizational context in which they will operate. Teams at different stages of AI maturity will apply these practices differently: a team that has operated AI systems in production for two years has a richer base of operational experience to inform their implementation choices than a team deploying their first agent. Neither team should simply adopt a template; both should evaluate each practice against their specific risk profile, operational constraints, and team capabilities.

Cross-functional collaboration is essential for sustainable implementation. Engineering, AI safety, product, and SRE teams all have legitimate interests in how these practices are designed and enforced. Practices designed without input from the teams who must live with them tend to be either too abstract to apply consistently or too prescriptive to accommodate the real variability in situations they will encounter. Invest in the collaborative design process upfront; the time spent building shared understanding saves time in adoption and reduces the resistance that comes from practices imposed without consultation.

Measurement is the foundation of continuous improvement. Each practice described here has an associated metric: review cycle time, DoD completion rate, eval pass rate, incident rate, checklist failure distribution. Teams that track these metrics and review them regularly can identify whether their implementations are achieving their intended effects, where friction is causing workarounds, and where investment in tooling or capacity would provide the highest return. Practices that are not measured are not improved; they either remain static while the environment changes around them or gradually decay as their original purpose is forgotten.

Finally, recognize that the practices described here are means, not ends. The ends are: agent capabilities that work reliably, behave safely, can be operated and maintained by the team, and create genuine value for users. Every practice should be evaluated against these outcomes. When a practice is creating overhead without demonstrably contributing to these outcomes, it should be reformed or replaced. When a practice is clearly preventing incidents or improving quality, it should be maintained even when it creates friction. The discipline of outcome-oriented engineering process management is what separates teams that maintain effective practices long-term from teams whose practices gradually become compliance exercises.
Semantic versioning provides a contract: PATCH = safe to upgrade, MINOR = backward-compatible additions, MAJOR = breaking changes.
Publish EOL dates at the time of major version release so teams can plan migration work in advance.
Deprecation requires runtime warnings in library code plus a migration guide; changelog entries alone are insufficient.
Maintenance support (security patches only) provides a bridge window between major versions — typically six months.
Short, enforced support windows are more operationally sustainable than open-ended extension policies.
Measure version distribution across the fleet and publish it; visibility creates upgrade momentum.
